content.full_text:
Re
Farn PolrrrcAt- PRACTTcES CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0586
June 9,2004
Vona Copp, Treasurer
Nakanishi for Assernbly 2O04.
8958Ivanpah Court
Elk Grove, CA95624
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-04-105
Dear Ms. Copp:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf ofNakanishi for
Assembly 2OO4 for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act').' Sit ce you are asking for advicebn the general application of the law, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistancepursuant to regulation 18329(c).
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunityprovided by an
opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy anclosed.)
Nothing in this letter shall be construed to comment on any conduct which has already
occurred.
QUESTIONS
l. May funds on hand in the Nakanishi for Assembly 2004 committee be used to
pay the debts of the following:
(a) Nakanishi for Assembly 2002 (formed for the Assernbly member's current
term of office).
(b) Nakanishi for Assembly 1998 (Pre-Proposition 34)
(c) Nakanishi for Senate (Pre-Proposition 34).
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear. at Title 2, sections 181@-18997, of the
Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. I-04-105
Page No. 2
2. After the November 20A4 election, what limitations does the Act impose on
the funds rernaining in Nakanishi for Assembly 2N4?
CONCLUSIONS
l.(a) Nakanishifor Assembly 2002 (formedfor the Assembly member's carrent
term of ffice).' Section 85306(a) allows candidates to transfer fimds from one conholled
committee to another of his or her controlled committees for elective state office. The
funds must be attributed to contributors of the committee making the transfer using a
"last in, first out" or "first in, first out" accounting method and the total hansferred funds
athibuted to a particular contributor cannot.exceed the amount that person actually
contributed-
l. (b) and (c) Nalanishifor Assembly 1998 and Nakanishifor Senate (Pre-
Proposition j4): Neither of the two committees receiving funds is subject to the
contribution limits of sections 85301 and 85302- Since this is the case, attribution is not
required and fimds can be transferred from Nakanishi for Assembly ZO@-lo these
committees without limit- Given that attribution is not applicablg contributors to the
2004 committee may not make replacement contributions to the 2004 committee,
ensuring there is no evasion of the contribution limits applicable to the 2004 election.
These funds, once transferred, \Mill be surplus frmds subject to section 89519, as
discussed below.
2. Assuming you win the November 2004 election and havefunds remaining any
expenditure of campaign funds held in Nakanishi for Assembly 2ffi must comport with
the "personal use" law. The funds do not become "surplus funds" subject to greater
restriction until you leave office.
FACTS
Assemblymernber Nakanishi has three committees with outstanding debt from
previous elections. Nakanishi forAssembly 1998, apre-Proposition 34 committee, has
$66,500 in loans due to Alan Nakanishi, Nakanishi for Senate, also apre-Proposition 34
committee, has $51,000 in loans to Alan Nakanishi and an additional loan of $5,000
owed to Vellutini Corporation. The third committee, Nakanishi forAssembly2002 has
loans of $93,000 due to Alan Nakarrishi and $2,000 due to Pat Bates for Asseinbly-
Assemblymember Nakanishi wishes to use funds on hand in the 2004 committee
to pay the debts of these old committees.


File No. I-04-105
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
Introduction: One Bank Account Rule
ln June 1988, Proposition 73 was approved by the voters as amendments to the
Act. Among other thirgt, Proposition 73 enacted section 85201, which required that all
conhibutions or loans made to a candidate, or to the candidate's controlled committee,
had to be deposited into a single campaign bank account. This section came to be known
as the "one-bank account" rule. Proposition 73 requirod the following:
Section 85201 provided that all contributions or loans made to a candidate, or to the
candidate's controlled committee, had to be deposited in a single campaign bank
account.
o
a
o
Section 85201(e) provided that all campaign expenditmes had to be made from the
appropriate campaign bank account.
Section 85202(b) provided that contributions deposited into the campaign bank
account must be used only for expenses associated with the election ofthe candidate
to the specific office which the candidate intended to seek or expenses associated
with holding that office.2
Thus, under the one-bank account rule, contributions raised fora specific election must
be deposited in the account established for that election, and expenditmes cannot be made
from the 2004 committee to pay the expenses of any other committeg except as set forth
in regulation 18525, which provides a limited exception to this nrle for officeholder
expenditures. Regulation 18525 addresses incrmrbent candidates' election expenses and
officeholder expenses. Regulation 18525 (copy enclosed) sets forth the kinds of
expenditures which mustbemade from accounts for election to future office by
incumbent elected officers, and allows other expemditures to be made from either the
officeholder account (the Assembly mernber's 2002 account) or the future election
account (2004). Thus, pursuant to regulation 18525(b), the Assemblymembermayuse
his 2004 reelection account to pay for officeholder expenses associated with holding that
officewonin 20U. (Seealso, HiltachkAdiceLetter,4-04-006; DannerAdviceLetter,
No. A-96-109.) However, payment of outstanding campaign debts owed by another
campaign committee would not be considered an officeholder expense and direct
payment from the 2004 commiuee would not be permitted.
2 As conceived, Proposition 73 prohibited a candidate from transferring contributions directly or
indirectly among his or her various campaign bank accounts. On Septembe r 25, 1990, the United States
Dishict Court in Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices
Commission invalidated portions of the Act added by Proposition 73, including the fiscal year conkibution
limitations and the ban on inter- and inha-candidate transfers. However, despite a candidate's ability to
tansfer campaign funds among his or her own campaign bank accounts, the "one-bank account" rule
continued to prohibit more than one bank accountper election.


FileNo. I-04-105
Page No. 4
Question la Transfer to Nakanishifor Assembly 2002 (formedfor the Assembly
member's cufient term of ofjice).
Proposition 34, passed by the voters in the Novernber 2000 general election,
imposed conhibution limits on candidates for elective state office. (Sections 85300-
8532L).3 However, section S5306(a) continues to allow candidates to transf€r funds
from one controlled committee to another ofhis or her controlled committees for elective
state office under certain circumstances.n The funds must be attributed to specific
contributors to the committee making the transfer using a "last in, first out" or "first in,
first out" accounting method. Implicit in the concept of attribution is the requirement that
the total transferred funds attributed to a particulm contributor cannot exceed the amount
that person actually contributed. (Regulation 185360).) However, once the fimds of a
contributor to the 2002[ committee have been transferred, this contributor is able to
contribute againup to the applicable limit.
For example, appllng the nrle in the context of a $3,200 contribution limit, if the
contributorgave $2,000 to the 2W cnmmittee, all ofwhich is transferred tothe2OO2
committee, that contributor can give $3,200 to the 2004 committee. On the other hand, if
only $1,000 of that contributor's contributions are hansferred to the 2002 committee,
then $1,000 of that contributor's contributions remain in the 2004 committee, and only
$2,200 could be contributed by that contributor to the 2004 committee.
Questions 1(b) and (c). Transfer to Nahanishifor Assembly 1995 ond Nakanishifor
Senate.
Neither of the two committees receiving funds is subject to the contribution limits
of sections 85301 and 85302. (Section 85321.) Since this is the casq attribution is not
required and funds can be transferred from Nakanishi forAssembly 2Af4bthese
committees without limit.5 Please note, however, that since attribution is not utilized,
new contributions cannot be received from the sarne contributors into the 2004 commiftee
to replenish the transferred funds. When campaign funds are transferred into these old
3 Contribution limits may increase or decrease e.rrcql two years based on changes in the Consumer
Price Index. The current limit for Assembly candidates is $6,400 from small contnlbutor cornmittees, and
$3,200 from all other persons. Political parties are not limited in how much tlrey may contribute to
Assembly candidates-
a Section 85318 has additional restrictions on the use of campaigrr funds raised for a general
election. For example, while section 85318 allows canlidates to raise general election and primary election
funds simultaneously, it also provides: 'If tle candidate for elective state office is defeatedin the primary
election or special primary election, or otlerwise withdraws from the general election or special general 
-
election, the general election or special general election funds shall be refunded to the conhibutors on a pro
rata basis less any expenses associated with the raising and administation of general election or special
general election conkibutions." However, this section is not triggered since you were successful in the
primary election.
t However, as ofJanuary 24,z}O4,post-election fund-raising (regardless ofthe date ofthe
election for which the firnds are being raised) is limited by section 85316 to outstanding net debt.
(Regulation 18531.61.)


File No. I-04-105
Page No. 5
accounts, these funds become surplus and may only be used forpurposes set forth in
section 89519, including repayment of debt, as discussed below.
In contrast to the example set forth in question l(a) above, in this case money
transferred to the old committee is not attributed to anyone and therefore no contributor's
funds are rernoved from the 2004 committee. Consequently, there is no change with
respect to those persons who contributed to the 2004 committee. For examplq if a
contributor gave $1,000 to the 2004 committee, that contributor could only give an
additional $2,200 to the 2004 committee. None ofthe original $1,000 can be considered
to have been transferred out.
Question 2. Use of Funds in the NokanishiforAssemb$ 2A04 Committce after the
Election
Assuming you win the November election and hwe lefioverfunds remaininlB, the
use of the funds will be subject to the Act's provisions conceming thepermissible uses of
campaign funds (sections 8951G'89522)-T'be general rule is that absent an express
provision pertaining to the expenditure, any expexrditure of campaign funds must be, at a
minimum, reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
Where campaign firnds are "surplus," strictetr rules apply- Section 89519 provides
that "[u]pon leaving any elected officq or at the end of the postelection reporting period
following the defeat of a candidate for elective office, whichever occurs lasl" funds held
by a defeated candidate me considered "surplus" campaign fimds and may only be used
for certain restricted purposes. Assuming you win in November, your funds will not be
considered surplus until after you leave office. Under sertion 89519, a candidate may
only spend surplus fimds on the following expenses:
Payment of outstanding campaign debts or officeholder expenses.
Repayrnent of contributions.
Donations to any bona fide charitable, educational, civic, religious, or similar tax-
exempt, nonprofit organization, if no substantial part ofthe proceeds will have a
material financial effect on the candidatg any member of his or her immediate
family, or the campaign treasurer. '
Contributions to a political party or committeg as long as the funds are not used to
make contributions in support of or in opposition to a candidate for elective office.
Conkibutions to support or oppose any candidate for federal office, any candidate in
another state, or any ballot measure.
Professional services, such as legal or accounting services reasonably required by the
committee to assist in its administrative functions.
o
o
o
a
a


File No. I-04-105
Page No. 6
o
Enclosures
LM:JWWjg
I:\Adviceltrs\04-105
fiw
A home or office security systern, if the candidate has received threats to his or her
physical safety and other conditions are met.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
,John W- Wallace
Assistant General Counsel,
Iegal Division
